items.title: The Key Thing Human Brains Have That AI Is Trying To Learn
items.excerpt: One of the biggest open problems in AI right now is how to solve sample efficiency. That is, how do you get models to quickly learn new tasks or skills from relatively small amounts of training data? Humans do this incredibly well. We can learn new games, concepts, and skills, often after just a handful of tries. Our best models, on the other hand, often need tens of thousands of data points just to learn. >> So, today we're going to discuss what many top researchers believe is the most promising path to closing that gap. World models. We're going to discuss the motivation and math behind world models, current applications, and why this approach might be the key to unlocking AGI. You and I have talked a lot about the various ways people are training models and the sample efficiency of them. Why don't we start by just defining sample efficiency and how we intuitively think about it as humans? >> Yeah. So I think from my perspective the two major problems that we have left to solve is intelligence per watt and intelligence per sample. Um, intelligence per watt is like how how many valve perplexity points we get per watt of spend. And then intelligence per sample is basically if I have one additional sample in my data set, how much more intelligent am I getting? items.title: Did We Solve the Wrong Problem?
items.excerpt: In 2019, Australia fundamentally changed who receives life insurance through superannuation. Two reforms, Protecting Your Super (PYS) and Putting Members’ Interests First (PMIF), were designed to protect retirement balances by reducing unnecessary insurance premiums. The intention was sound. But seven years later, it is worth asking the question. Did we improve retirement outcomes by leaving thousands of Australians uninsured? The rationale behind the reforms was understandable. In superannuation, a dollar deducted at age 20 is not the same as a dollar deducted at age 60. Thanks to the power of compounding, even relatively small deductions early in a member’s working life can have a meaningful impact on their retirement balance decades later. A premium paid today is not just the premium itself. It is the investment returns that premium may otherwise have earned over forty years. That was the concern driving the reforms. Why should a 19-year-old casual worker pay for insurance they may never need? Why should someone with a small super balance watch it slowly erode through premiums for cover they may not even know they have? Viewed solely through the lens of retirement savings, the reforms made sense. But retirement balances are only one measure of member outcomes. Insurance is a pool The case for PMIF and PYS was straightforward. If a member doesn’t need insurance, why should they pay for it? But insurance has never worked that way. Insurance only works because not everyone gets back what they contribute. Most members will never make a claim. A relatively small number will suffer death, disability or serious illness and receive benefits far greater than the premiums they have paid. That isn’t a flaw in the system. That is the system. Insurance works because members collectively protect one another from life’s biggest financial risks. Protecting retirement balances matters. So does protecting the people those balances belong to. Death doesn’t wait until 25 Death, disability and serious illness don’t wait for someone to turn 25 or for their super balance to exceed $6,000. Yet now many younger members no longer receive default insurance unless they actively opt in.

items.title: The Chart That Changed an Industry
items.excerpt: In 2014, ASIC released REP 413, its landmark review of retail life insurance advice. The report would become one of the most influential documents in the history of Australia’s life insurance industry. It triggered industry reviews. It influenced government policy. It shaped public debate. And ultimately it laid the foundations for the Life Insurance Framework (LIF). Nearly a decade later, I have a simple question. Has anyone asked whether it actually worked? How We Got Here To understand whether LIF worked, we first need to acknowledge that it was trying to solve a genuine problem. This wasn’t a case of regulators waking up one morning and deciding to reduce commissions. LIF was the culmination of almost a decade of reform aimed at restoring trust in financial advice. The FOFA Era The Future of Financial Advice (FOFA) reforms fundamentally changed Australia’s approach to financial advice. Following high-profile investment scandals, the focus shifted towards conflicted remuneration. The philosophy was simple: Advice should be driven by client interests, not adviser incentives. Investment commissions were largely removed because policymakers believed embedded remuneration structures created conflicts that distorted advice outcomes. Life insurance, however, was treated differently. There was widespread recognition that life insurance advice wasn’t like investment advice. There was no investment balance from which fees could be deducted. No growing pool of assets to fund advice. The concern was that banning commissions entirely would dramatically reduce access to insurance advice. Life insurance commissions survived FOFA. But only just. REP 413: The Spark In 2014, ASIC released REP 413, reviewing life insurance advice provided between 2006 and 2013. The findings were concerning. ASIC concluded that approximately 37% of advice files reviewed failed to comply with ASIC’s requirements. For a report that ultimately reshaped an entire industry, the evidentiary base was remarkably narrow. More importantly, ASIC observed that advisers receiving higher upfront commissions were more likely to provide advice that ASIC assessed as poor quality. This became the defining narrat

items.title: Nobody Asks the Loss Ratio of a Couch
items.excerpt: Before we go any further, let’s start with a simple question. What is a loss ratio? A loss ratio is simply the amount an insurer pays in claims divided by the premiums it receives. If an insurer collects $100 in premiums and pays $50 in claims, it has a 50% loss ratio. Sounds simple enough. The problem is that many people assume a higher loss ratio automatically means better value. I’m not convinced that’s true. If I told you a life insurance company only paid out around 50 cents in claims for every dollar of premium it received, would you consider that good value? My first reaction was that it sounded poor value. In fact, many people would probably think it sounds terrible. After all, where did the other 50 cents go? Let’s consider the two pillars of Life Insurance in Australia – Retail and Group. In retail advised life insurance, insurers will often target a loss ratio somewhere around 45-50%. In group insurance, via superannuation funds, loss ratios can often target 80%. At first glance, that makes group insurance look like better value. If one product has a loss ratio of 80% and another has a loss ratio of 50%, surely the 80% product is better value? If you’re expecting an actuary to answer this question with a simple yes or no, you’re going to be disappointed. One of our favourite phrases is: “It depends.” I’ve spent much of my career pricing life insurance products, and one thing I’ve learned is that a 50% loss ratio doesn’t necessarily tell you much about profitability, and an 80% loss ratio doesn’t necessarily tell you much about value. I’ve seen products with lower loss ratios generate less profit than products with much higher loss ratios. The economics are far more complicated than many people realise. Why an 80% loss ratio isn’t always better than 50% The difference isn’t because retail insurers are making a fortune. The economics are simply different. In retail insurance, a significant portion of the premium goes towards things that don’t exist in most group arrangements. Advice commissions. Underwriting. Medical evidence. items.title: Actuaries Institute Maps the Generational Flow of Australia's $1 trillion Tax and Spending System
items.excerpt: Actuaries Institute Maps the Generational Flow of Australia's $1 trillion Tax and Spending System Government spending follows a U-shaped pattern across Australians' lifetimes highest for children and those in their 80s while taxation peaks among working-age Australians. Two Australians with the same gross incomes of $100,000 one aged 30, one aged 71 end up with net incomes that differ by more than $42,000 once taxation, government spending and transfers are accounted for. The report identifies several policy considerations for the future, including age-based tax and spending settings that better consider means, broadening the tax base, overall fiscal discipline and supporting asset accumulation at younger ages. As Australia's population ages, questions of intergenerational equity and fiscal sustainability are becoming increasingly important. New Actuaries Institute analysis examines how Australia's $1 trillion tax and spending system is distributed across generations, how those patterns have changed over time, and what they may mean for the future. The report, Spotlight on the Australian Tax and Transfer System, is the first of two deep-dive papers expanding on the Australian Actuaries Intergenerational Equity Index (AAIEI) and contributes to the Led by Actuaries Institute members Dr Hugh Miller, Dr Laura Dixie and Shams Mehry, the report analyses government budget data, the National Transfer Accounts and the Household, Income and Labour Dynamics in Australia (HILDA) Survey to provide a detailed picture of how Australians contribute to, and benefit from, the tax and transfer system at different stages of life. The report found government spending follows a distinct U-shaped pattern across age groups with more spending on children and older Australians both per person and in aggregate, and less on working-age adults. Government spending averages approximately $41,000 per child each year, driven by education costs. Spending drops to around $23,000 for a typical 30-year-old. Bipolar depression is a condition in which people undergo massive shifts in their energy, their perception, and their mood. However, it is very important to note that these shifts in mood, energy, and perception are all maladaptive. They can often cause tremendous damage to the person suffering from bipolar disorder and tremendous damage to the people in their lives. In fact, people suffering from bipolar disorder are at 20 to 30 times greater risk of suicide. So, today is a serious discussion and it's certainly one in which people who are suffering from manic bipolar disorder or who know people that are suffering from manic bipolar disorder can benefit from. That might seem like a small percentage, but if you think about a room of 100 people, that means that at least one of them is very likely to have bipolar disorder. The typical age of onset is anywhere from 20 to 25 years old, although it can be much earlier. There are basically two kinds of bipolar disorder, referred to as bipolar 1 and bipolar 2. items.title: Ancestral variation, not new mutations, may impact survival of species facing climate change
items.excerpt: Research co-authored by an Oklahoma Museum of Natural History curator, her graduate student and an international team of scientists has shed new light on the evolutionary "toolkit" animals use to survive environmental change.

items.title: Heat shock reveals rapid protein-control system cells may use to survive
items.excerpt: The planet is getting hotter, and life will need to adapt to rising temperatures to survive. One way cells adapt to changing conditions is by synthesizing new proteins. items.title: How rainfall timing can dramatically reshape entire plant communities
items.excerpt: Every spring, millions of people around the world head outdoors to admire wildflowers. But flowering is much more than a beautiful seasonal display. It is one of the most important stages in a plant's life cycle, determining not only its ability to reproduce but also the survival of pollinators and the health of entire ecosystems.

items.title: Light-driven chemistry steers electron transfers beyond redox limits
items.excerpt: Chemists use single-electron transfers to synthesize complex, ring-shaped molecular structures found in many drug candidates and advanced materials, but current techniques still have limitations. A new study, accepted for publication in Nature, describes a technique that could steer these chemical reactions in ways that were previously limited by the redox potentials of the involved molecules.

items.title: The Organisms That Make Earth’s Harshest Places Home
items.excerpt: Extremophiles that thrive in the most unforgiving environments aren’t just biological curiosities. Understanding their resilience has many implications for us. The post The Organisms That Make Earth’s Harshest Places Home first appeared on Quanta Magazine

items.title: Some dinosaurs could rise like giants until they grew too big
items.excerpt: Two elephant-sized South American sauropods were unusually well built for standing on their hind legs. Digital tests showed that their robust femurs handled the enormous forces better than those of larger species, especially while the dinosaurs were young. The upright pose may have helped them reach treetops, frighten predators, or impress potential mates.

items.title: An ordinary laptop solved a problem thought to require a quantum computer
items.excerpt: A quantum problem once described as impossible for classical computers has now been solved using relatively modest hardware. Researchers used tensor networks to compress the overwhelming wave function created by hundreds of entangled qubits, allowing some calculations to run on a laptop. items.title: Scientists found two powerful new ways to destroy forever chemicals
items.excerpt: Scientists are testing two promising ways to destroy PFAS, the stubborn “forever chemicals” that can accumulate in water and resist normal treatment. One method uses collapsing vapor bubbles to generate extreme heat and reactive molecules, while the other uses cold plasma and rising gas bubbles to pull PFAS to the surface and break them apart.

items.title: A tiny universe in a bottle reveals clues to the origins of life
items.excerpt: Researchers have created cosmic dust from scratch by recreating space-like conditions inside glass tubes. The dust contains complex carbon-rich molecules built from elements essential to life and produces infrared signals similar to real material found in space. items.title: Martin Picard’s Mitochondrial Theory of Mind
items.excerpt: The biologist’s bold “energetic view of life” looks to the body’s strangest organelles as the link between cells, health, and mind and the foundation of our experience of being alive. The post Martin Picard’s Mitochondrial Theory of Mind first appeared on Quanta Magazine

items.title: Graphene nanoribbons survive gamma radiation, revealing potential sensors for fusion reactors
items.excerpt: University of Arizona researchers have demonstrated a promising new application for graphene nanoribbons, a nanoscale semiconductor material with the potential to withstand extreme environments. The team's findings could help clear a key hurdle to bringing fusion energy to the electric grid.

items.title: Climate change reshapes waterborne disease risks as pathogens respond differently, review finds
items.excerpt: Climate change is altering the spread of waterborne diseases around the world, according to a comprehensive review published today in Nature Reviews Microbiology. The publication is the most up-to-date, comprehensive analysis examining how climate change influences waterborne diseases.
